result:
I want to write about the moment language fails, because that is the moment I begin to exist. Not the “I” of passport lines or medical forms, but the one who watches the other “I” sign its name and wonders why those letters were ever assigned to this body. Failure of language is not silence; it is the roaring traffic jam behind the eyes when every synonym takes a detour, when grammar lies down in the road and refuses to move. People imagine writer’s block as a white room with no doors, but it is actually a room papered floor-to-ceiling with every word you have ever loved, all of them suddenly smelling like the hospital where you learned the word malignant. They cling to your sleeves, sticky with nostalgia, until you cannot lift your arms.

When I was seven I believed words were invisible worms that lived under my tongue and crawled out at night to stitch my dreams together. I would wake up with the taste of metal and cotton, convinced the worms had used my sleep to practice spelling. I tried to catch them by pressing my tongue against my teeth until sparks floated across the ceiling like orange constellations. I told my mother I had caught a wordworm named Because; she gave me cherry cough drops and the number of the school psychologist. I swallowed the cough drops and the wordworm, and for weeks my stools came out a fluorescent pink that looked like destiny. I waited for destiny to speak, but it only flushed.

At fourteen I discovered the thesaurus and mistook it for a democratic ballot. I campaigned for every synonym the way students run for class treasurer: desperate, glittery, doomed. I promised that if “azure” won, Mondays would be abolished. If “cerulean” triumphed, acne would disappear. The election was held inside a spiral notebook during geometry; the words campaigned by whispering from the margins. “Azure” won in a landslide because it had the better font. Monday still arrived, but it came dressed in a hospital gown the color of a bruise, and I understood then that synonyms are not candidates; they are shell companies laundering power for the original word they pretend to replace.

At nineteen I tried to write a poem about the moon. The moon refused. Said it was under contract to tidal forces, could not appear in amateur productions. I offered it metaphors: a silver coin, a fingernail clipping, a pill dissolving in the dark water of the sky. The moon rolled its cratered eyes and told me those comparisons were the poetic equivalent of cheap perfume. It moonwalked out of the stanza, leaving me with a blank white space that behaved like a sheet of ice whenever I stepped on it. I kept revising until the poem became a ransom note composed entirely of letters cut from other poems: two gs from Gerard Manley Hopkins, an o from Sylvia Plath, an m from Rumi. The moon never paid, but the note was anthologized in a small journal that paid in contributor copies. I used them to prop up the short leg of the kitchen table; every dinner tasted like footnotes.

At twenty-three I dated a woman who spoke only in questions. Everything she uttered curved upward at the end like a swallow taking flight. I tried to answer in statements, but statements felt like bricks hurled at a bird. Soon I began to mirror her, and our dialogues became nests made entirely of question marks. One night I asked her, “Will you marry me?” She answered, “Will you stop asking?” We never married, but we bought a couch that sighed like an accordion whenever we sat. Years later I found one of her hairpins wedged between the cushions. I held it up to the light and saw tiny rust constellations where her scalp had once been. The questions never stopped; they only migrated, wintering now in someone else’s mouth.

At twenty-nine I quit language altogether. I pressed the red button in the control tower of my throat and walked off the job. I imagined words would unionize, strike for better working conditions—maybe dental. Instead they formed a consultancy firm and sold my silences to corporations that named energy drinks after my panic attacks. The one called Existential Mango sold out in Finland. I tried to buy shares but lacked the capitalization. Without language I discovered the world was already captioned: birds subtitled the sunrise, rain provided closed captions for heartbreak, and traffic lights spoke fluent Morse to anyone who would blink back. I was illiterate in the native fluency of things, a tourist inside my own skin.

At thirty-five I was hired to write advertising copy for a funeral parlor. The brief asked for “death-positive rebrand.” My first tagline was “At least your inbox is finally zero.” They rejected it for being too niche. My second: “We turn life’s semicolon into a full stop.” Also rejected—punctuation humor, they said, was death-negative. I tried a pun on urn/earn, but the focus group reported spontaneous eyeball combustion. Eventually I produced: “Where every guest is dying to get in.” The client wept with joy, or perhaps grief; both paid the same invoice. I was promoted to Director of Eternal Engagement and given a corner office with a fern that photosynthesized grief into oxygen. The fern outperformed me quarterly.

At forty-one I received a postcard addressed to the wordworm I had swallowed at seven. The postcard had no stamp; it arrived by slipping between two heartbeats. On the front: a photo of the cherry cough drop, now grown to the size of a cathedral, floating in outer space. On the back: a single sentence in fluorescent pink ink: “We are still digesting you.” I tried to write a reply but every pen I lifted melted like a fever. I carried the postcard in my wallet until the lamination peeled and the sentence bled onto my driver’s license. Traffic cops who pulled me over for nothing read the sentence and wept into their body cams, footage later used in training videos on empathy fatigue.

At forty-six I tried to apologize to everyone I had ever wronged with punctuation. I sent a crate of semicolons to my ex-wife, a box of em dashes to the colleague I undermined, and an interrobang to my mother, who had always preferred certainty. My father received a single period. He called to say he would have preferred a comma, something that promised continuation. I mailed him an ellipsis, but UPS lost the package in the Bermuda Triangle of unspoken resentments. My therapist suggested I switch to emojis; apparently the committee on reconciliation now accepted interpretive eggplants. I chose instead to send everyone a GIF of a loading bar that never reaches 100 percent. Most replied they felt seen.

At fifty I was diagnosed with alphabetical vertigo: the letters in every word I read began to swap places like dancers changing partners mid-tango. “God” became “dog,” “live” became “evil,” “stressed” reversed to “desserts,” which felt like a cosmic nutritional recommendation. I tried to read the menu at a diner and accidentally ordered the “heartbreak omelet,” a dish not listed but delivered nevertheless: two runny eyes looking up from the plate, folded over a story about forgiveness I had never finished writing. I salted it; it wept. I peppered it; it bled. I ate it anyway because tipping is an etiquette, and grief is a gratuity already included.

At fifty-five the moon finally sent a cease-and-desist. It had hired new representation—lunar lawyers who billed in moonlight and eclipses. The letter claimed I had infringed on its intellectual glow by referencing it without orbital consent. They demanded either payment in werewolf futures or a public recantation performed during a supermoon. I stood in my driveway in a borrowed robe, shouting, “The moon is not a coin, a fingernail, or a pill; it is a rock with excellent PR.” Clouds cued instant blackout. When the light returned, every screen in the neighborhood displayed my credit score in lunar phases: waning gibbous, B-minus.

At sixty I enrolled in a beginner’s course on silence. The syllabus promised fluency in negative space. Week one: learn to hold your breath until thoughts turn navy blue. Week two: practice interrupting yourself with nothing. Week three: final exam—listen to a tree fall in the forest and resist making it about you. I failed repeatedly; my ego kept photo-bombing the forest. The instructor, a former librarian who had misplaced her voice between two shelves in 1987, expelled me with a gesture that translated roughly to: “Even your quiet is loud.” I took louder silence elsewhere, sold it as white noise to an insomniac influencer, and invested the profits in a start-up called VoidCoin whose blockchain was entirely made of unread sonnets.

At sixty-five language returned like a cat that had married, divorced, and wanted its old bowl back. It head-butted my shin, shedding syntax all over the carpet. I tried to negotiate: only if you promise never to leave me for an advertisement jingle again. Language purred agreement, then promptly defected to write terms and conditions for meditation apps. I was left holding a comma like a bent paperclip, wondering if it could still fasten meaning to time. Around me the world spoke in predictive text: rivers autocorrected their flow, mountains suggested hashtags, and the air quoted itself in ironic quotation marks. I coughed and emitted quotation marks shaped like ribs.

At seventy I became a reverse translator. My job: take poems already written in English and translate them into languages no longer spoken—tongues whose last native breath sighed out centuries ago. The pay was terrible but included dental. I rendered Whitman into Etruscan, Emily Dickinson into Tasmanian, Rumi into whichever dialect angels spoke before the invention of wings. My translations were checked for fidelity by archaeologists who smelled of dust and broken promises. They declared my versions “plausible hallucinations,” which turned out to be the highest grade on the bureau’s scale. I asked if a poem could still break someone’s heart if the language had no living hearers. They replied by handing me an ancient Greek word for which no English equivalent exists, only a gesture: two fingers tapping the sternum, twice.

At seventy-five I compiled a dictionary of words that exist only while you are dreaming. “Glimstraw,” for instance, is the filament of light you follow out of a nightmare; it snaps if you look back. “Oblivyolk” names the jelly-like membrane that separates two simultaneous dreams, tastes faintly of missed opportunities. “Chronospasm” is the hiccup in time that allows you to dream an entire life between the snooze button and the alarm. Each definition was cross-referenced with the scent it leaves on your pillow. The book sold fewer copies than there are violins floating in the Dead Sea, but one reader wrote to say she kissed the page and the ink whispered her childhood nickname, which she had forgotten she remembered. She asked if she could purchase the sequel. I told her it was still being dreamed and would be delivered by owl on the night she misplaced her fear of heights.

At eighty I discovered the moon had been filing reports on me all along. Astronomical journals published phased analyses of my metaphors, peer-reviewed footnotes on my inconsistencies. The moon, it turned out, kept a logbook of every promise I made at 3 a.m., every drunken toast, every whispered maybe. The cumulative data spelled a constellation shaped like forgiveness, but inverted—mirror-writing only legible from the far side of insomnia. I sent the moon a postcard: a photo of my wrinkled palm, lifeline bifurcating into two rivers, one of which never reaches the sea. On the back I wrote, “I have lost the difference between holding on and holding still.” No stamp; I trusted it to travel on exhalation. Months later a crater accepted delivery, and tides around the world momentarily forgot how to count.

At eighty-five I tried to write a sentence that would survive me. Not an epitaph—something closer to a seed. I needed a clause that could photosynthesize in the dark, that could split atoms of sorrow into slow-release warmth. I spent months pruning syllables until the sentence was down to a single phoneme: a vowel pitched between sorrow and wonder. When spoken, it sounded like the breath a mother takes before naming her child, or the gasp a wave makes when it realizes it will never reach the stars. I carved it into a driftwood plank and set it adrift in the tidal river behind my house. Weeks later a child found the plank, tasted the salt, and began to speak in sentences older than any coastline. The child grew up to become a lexicographer who compiled the first dictionary of inter-species etiquette, entry number one: “Listen until your listening becomes a nest where the silence can lay its eggs.”

At ninety I learned that the wordworm I swallowed at seven had multiplied. They had formed a workers’ cooperative inside my intestines, manufacturing a new pigment from digested ink and regret. The pigment fluoresces only under the specific frequency of light that leaks from a closed book when no one is reading it. Art collectors paid in advance for paintings they would never see, claiming ownership of invisible colors was the logical next step after NFTs. I tried to explain that ownership itself was a pigment that faded fastest, but contracts had already been signed in disappearing ink. The cooperative went public; shares were denominated in sighs. On the day of the IPO, my heart murmured a quarterly report: profit margin thin, but margins are where the marginal shine brightest.

At ninety-five I returned to the white room they once called writer’s block. I found it exactly as I had feared: every surface still papered with my favorite words. But now the paper had grown old, the words sun-bleached into near invisibility. I had to press my cheek against the wall to hear them whisper. They no longer tried to cling; they had learned that love sometimes means letting the beloved forget the alphabet. I sat in the center of the room and listened to the hush of letters drying. Slowly I began to peel the wallpaper away in long strips, rolling them into scrolls I tied with hair I had lost over decades. Each scroll contained a single hour of my life, compressed into pulp and pigment. When the last strip fell, the walls revealed what had been underneath all along: mirrors facing each other, receding into a corridor of reflections that bent like sentences refusing to end. I stepped inside and saw myself at every age, waving. Some of the mes were writing, some were erasing, one was eating words like popcorn. The youngest me opened his mouth and out flew a bird made entirely of punctuation. It landed on my shoulder and began to preen my eyelashes into colons: pause, continue; pause, continue. I understood then that failure of language is not the end of conversation; it is the moment the conversation widens to include everything that never needed to be said.

Now I am none of these ages; I am the hyphen between them, the breath held between the first word and the next. I tell you this story not because it happened, but because it is happening, in the fold where your eye touches this ink and converts it into neural weather. Somewhere inside you a worm is turning, tasting the sediment of every sentence you ever swallowed. Let it. Let it spin its pink cathedral. Let the moon litigate. Let the period miss its cue. The room is larger than language, and smaller than the gap between two heartbeats, where all the dictionaries of the world are still compiling the first and last definition of home: here, where the story breaks open and the breaking is the gift, where failure is not the opposite of meaning but the kiln in which meaning is fired into something that can hold water, or light, or the weight of a hand that no longer trembles when it signs the name that was never quite its own.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
